metadata.units: FII/DII values in Rs crore, net flow (positive = net buying/inflow, negative = net selling/outflow)

net_flow_score_scale.9_to_10: Very strong net institutional buying / high conviction
net_flow_score_scale.7_to_8: Moderate net buying / positive institutional bias

metadata.data_quality_note: FII/FPI sector-wise figures are built up from confirmed NSDL/depository and primeinfobase.com monthly and fortnightly sectoral flow reports for the relevant months (e.g. Dec25 H1, Feb26 full month, Mar26 H1, Apr26 H1, May26 full month) and summed/estimated into quarters; months or sectors without a directly reported figure are marked 'estimated' and built from the aggregate monthly FII total plus the sector's known relative share/direction that period. DII sector-wise splits are NOT separately published with the same granularity as FII/FPI data anywhere we could verify - DII figures here are directional estimates based on DII's well-documented role as a counter-cyclical offsetting buyer (typically absorbing 50-90% of FII selling in stress quarters, concentrated in BFSI/large-caps via SIP, insurance and pension inflows). Treat DII figures and all 'estimated' FII figures as approximate, not exact reported numbers. net_flow_score is our own qualitative 1-10 synthesis, not an official published metric.

drivers: Broad FII risk-off across EMs (Jul-Sep25 saw -Rs76,575cr aggregate FII selling)
BFSI's large index weight makes it the default sell-first sector for FIIs reducing India exposure
DII/mutual fund SIP buying partially offset the selling
FPIs pulled out ₹6,516 crore from financial services in H1 December 2025 alone, the largest sectoral outflow that fortnight
Brief Oct 2025 FII inflow (+₹14,610cr aggregate) gave BFSI a short reprieve before Nov-Dec selling resumed
DII buying (SIP, insurance, pension flows) continued to provide steady support
Financial services bore the brunt of the record March 2026 FPI outflow, with ₹31,831 crore sold in the first fortnight of March alone amid Iran-war/rupee stress
BFSI's high FPI ownership and index liquidity made it the preferred exit route for de-risking foreign funds
DII buying scaled up materially to cushion the fall, but could not fully offset the historic FII selling
BFSI absorbed ₹19,152 crore of the ₹48,141 crore total FPI outflow in H1 April 2026 (~40% of all selling) and ~70% (~$2,415mn) of May 2026's total FPI outflow
MSCI India EM weight decline (from ~20% in Sep24 to ~11.94% by Apr26) is mechanically forcing passive fund outflows concentrated in BFSI's large, liquid constituents
DII buying remained the key offsetting force keeping bank Nifty resilient despite heavy FII selling

drivers_expected: Government's LTCG tax waiver for FPIs on G-Secs (retroactive 1Apr26) aimed at stabilising flows, though this targets debt not equity directly
Q1FY27 private bank earnings (strong per recent commentary) could slow the pace of FII selling
MSCI EM passive-outflow pressure likely to persist but at a decelerating pace as rebalancing completes
If rupee/oil stress eases as the Iran-war situation stabilises, BFSI is typically the first sector where FII buying returns given its liquidity and index weight
Continued strong DII/SIP inflows expected to keep the sector well-supported regardless of FII direction

drivers: Ongoing tariff risk and global trade uncertainty kept FIIs cautious on US-revenue-dependent IT
'Long Asia - Short India' positioning by global funds awaiting Indo-US trade clarity
IT posted one of the largest sectoral outflows through Nov-Dec 2025 (₹3,331 crore sold in H1 December alone)
FPIs continued avoiding US-revenue-heavy sectors on tariff/policy uncertainty
IT remained the biggest laggard even during the Feb 2026 FII inflow rebound, with sectoral outflows of about $1.87 billion that month
Continued underweight positioning by global funds on India IT amid weak US corporate tech spending signals
IT selling moderated sharply to just -$142 million in H1 April 2026, far smaller than BFSI/Healthcare outflows that period
Global funds reallocating toward AI/semiconductor hardware plays in Taiwan/Korea/China rather than India IT services, per H1-April 2026 flow commentary
Sharp late-Q2 rally (Nifty IT +4.3% in the week of 13-17Jul26) suggests DII and some FII short-covering/rotation back into large-cap IT began in June

drivers_expected: Strong Q1FY27 earnings from Tech Mahindra, TCS and others (per recent weekly market data) could draw renewed FII interest after a prolonged underweight
Global AI capex acceleration ($750bn-plus hyperscaler spend guided for 2026) is an indirect long-term positive even for services-led Indian IT if it lifts enterprise tech budgets
Seasonally, Q3 (Oct-Dec) US client budget-flush quarters have historically supported IT sector sentiment
Continued monitoring of US recession/AI-disruption risk to IT services demand remains the key swing factor

drivers: Broad FII risk-off period weighed on cyclicals including autos
Domestic demand resilience provided some DII support
Auto was among sectors that saw a partial inflow return alongside the brief Oct26 FII buying spell before broader Nov-Dec selling resumed
Auto attracted selective FII buying interest in Feb 2026 (capital goods, auto, construction, metals led the ₹22,615cr inflow month)
But saw renewed selling of ₹4,807 crore during the record March 2026 outflow episode amid oil-price/input-cost concerns
Auto/auto-components sold off ₹3,704 crore in H1 April 2026 and further in May amid oil-driven input cost and demand concerns
Domestic demand (Hero MotoCorp-led rally, multiple positive sessions per weekly market data) kept DII buying steady

drivers_expected: Festive-season demand expectations typically draw both FII and DII interest into auto ahead of Q3
Monsoon performance and rural income recovery are key swing factors for two-wheeler/tractor demand
Festive season (Oct-Dec) sales momentum historically the strongest seasonal driver for the sector
Oil-price normalisation (if Iran-war tensions ease) would be a meaningful tailwind for auto input costs and consumer sentiment

drivers: IT, healthcare and chemicals faced FPI selling on ongoing tariff risk through this period
Healthcare recorded ₹2,351 crore of outflows in H1 December 2025 amid continued US-tariff-related caution on export-heavy pharma names
Healthcare saw ₹2,436 crore of outflows during the March 2026 selloff, though smaller in magnitude than BFSI/auto
Defensive character of the sector limited the scale of selling relative to cyclicals
Healthcare was one of the larger sectoral outflows in H1 April 2026 (₹4,481 crore) - the second-largest after BFSI that fortnight

Novo Nordisk's India regulatory approval for its Wegovy obesity drug (reported mid-Jul26) triggered a sharp sector-wide selloff in Nifty Pharma/Healthcare (-1.4%/-1.28% for the week) on competitive-threat concerns, dragging Dr Reddy's and Sun Pharma

drivers_expected: Continued overhang from the Wegovy-competition narrative likely to keep FIIs cautious near-term
Sector remains in the 'Leading' quadrant on longer-horizon relative rotation graphs, suggesting DII conviction buying on dips
Defensive rotation typically favours pharma/healthcare if broader market volatility (oil/geopolitics) persists into Q4
US generic-pricing and FDA-approval newsflow remain the key sector-specific swing factors

drivers: General EM risk-off weighed on richly-valued consumer staples
FMCG saw ₹1,419 crore of outflows in H1 December 2025, smaller than financials/IT/services that fortnight
FMCG recorded ₹2,403 crore of outflows during the March 2026 selloff as even defensive segments were not spared
FMCG saw ₹2,976 crore of outflows in H1 April 2026 and further selling in May, reflecting demand-compression risk from energy-price pass-through to consumers
Rural demand recovery hopes (linked to monsoon outlook) kept DII interest steady

drivers_expected: Monsoon progress and rural income trends are the key swing factor for FMCG volume growth expectations
Input cost relief if crude/edible oil prices ease would support margin narratives
Festive-season consumption demand typically supports FMCG sentiment into Q4
Continued monitoring of urban discretionary demand recovery

drivers: General FII de-risking period affected energy alongside most sectors
Oil & gas saw selective FII buying interest during the brief Oct 2025 inflow spell
Oil & gas recorded ₹2,932 crore of outflows in the March 2026 selloff even as global crude prices rose sharply, reflecting concern over margin compression at refiners/OMCs from higher input costs
Sector also drew some inflows in Feb 2026 as part of the capex/cyclical rotation basket
Oil & gas saw ₹3,352 crore of outflows in H1 April 2026 amid the West Asia conflict escalation

Reliance Industries' promoter stake increase to 50.48% and ~2.4% weekly gain (per recent market data) reflects selective stock-specific DII/domestic buying even as sector-wide FII sentiment stayed cautious
Rising crude prices are a double-edged sword: positive for upstream E&P names, negative for import-dependent refiners/OMCs

drivers_expected: Continued elevated crude prices (Iran-war linked) likely to keep upstream/E&P sentiment constructive even as OMC margins stay under pressure
Reliance Q1FY27 results are a key stock-specific catalyst for the sector
Any de-escalation in the Strait of Hormuz situation and oil-price normalisation would be a meaningful positive re-rating trigger for OMCs specifically

drivers: General FII selling period; global metals price softness added pressure
Selective buying interest during the brief Oct26 inflow window
Metals was one of the sectors, alongside capital goods and autos, that led the Feb 2026 FII inflow rebound (₹22,615cr month) on the domestic capex/cyclical rotation theme
Metals and Mining was one of only three sectors (with Services and Capital Goods) to attract net FPI inflows in May 2026, together pulling in about $1.75 billion
Mixed/negative weekly sessions in mid-July per recent market data suggest some profit-booking after the run-up

drivers_expected: Global metal price trends and China demand signals remain the key swing factor
Continued domestic capex/infrastructure theme supportive of steel/metals demand
Infrastructure capex execution (Union Budget-linked spend) typically supports metals demand into H2 of the fiscal year

drivers: General risk-off period; domestic capex story provided some DII cushioning
Heavy selling in capital goods (alongside power, cement, realty, telecom) by Dec 2025 pointed to rising FII concern about a slowing capex cycle
Capital Goods emerged as the single biggest beneficiary of the Feb 2026 FII inflow rebound, reflecting investor confidence in India's infrastructure/manufacturing expansion story
Capital Goods was one of the three sectors attracting net FPI inflows in May 2026 (with Services and Metals, ~$1.75bn combined)
Capital goods IIP output grew 12.9% YoY in May 2026, an unusually strong real-economy confirmation of the sector's momentum
CG Power's Rs900cr US data-centre transformer order and ABB India's record order backlog reflect genuine order-book strength underpinning sentiment

drivers_expected: Continued government infra capex execution (Rs11.21 lakh crore Budget 2026-27 allocation) supports the sector's medium-term order pipeline
Data-centre-linked power/automation capex (ABB, CG Power, Voltamp) is an emerging structural tailwind
H2-fiscal-year capex execution typically accelerates as government spending picks up into year-end

drivers: General risk-off period affected utilities alongside most sectors
Power saw ₹2,118 crore of outflows in H1 December 2025, among the larger sectoral outflows that fortnight, pointing to capex-cycle concerns
Power was among sectors leading the Feb 2026 FII inflow rebound alongside capital goods/metals/autos
Power was one of the only sectors with net FPI inflows in H1 April 2026 (+₹601 crore) even as most other sectors sold off
Electricity & gas supply output grew 9.9% YoY in May 2026 IIP data, reflecting genuine rising demand including data-centre-linked power consumption

drivers_expected: Data-centre power demand (flagged explicitly by ABB India management) and grid-modernisation capex are structural medium-term tailwinds
Renewable energy capacity additions continuing to draw both FII and DII interest
Continued grid-strengthening and data-centre-linked power infrastructure investment expected to sustain sector interest

drivers: Telecom was a relative safe-haven/inflow sector even during the broader Jul-Sep25 FII selling period, supported by 5G rollout and ARPU growth themes
Telecom saw notable selling by Dec 2025 (grouped with power, capital goods, cement, realty as capex-cycle-concern sectors)
Telecom recorded ₹3,856 crore of outflows during the record March 2026 FPI selloff
Telecom (combined with IT) saw a relatively modest -$267 million outflow in H1 April 2026, smaller than BFSI/Healthcare/Consumer Services that period
Tejas Networks' TJ1600-D3 launch and NEC 5G MIMO agreement provided stock-specific positive catalysts even as sector flows stayed negative overall

drivers_expected: ARPU growth and continued 5G/fibre rollout remain structural positives
Tejas Networks' rising losses despite revenue growth is a stock-specific risk to watch within the sector
Continued data-consumption growth and potential further ARPU hikes are the key sector tailwinds heading into year-end

drivers: General risk-off period; housing demand fundamentals remained resilient domestically
Realty was grouped among sectors (with power, capital goods, cement, telecom) flagged for capex-cycle-slowdown concerns by Dec 2025
Construction saw ₹2,975 crore of outflows during the March 2026 selloff
Sector still drew some Feb 2026 inflow interest as part of the capex/cyclical rotation basket
Realty led the prior week's rally before easing out of the weekly top-5 sector performers by mid-July per recent market data, suggesting momentum paring rather than a sharp reversal
Sector remains in the 'Leading' quadrant on longer-horizon relative rotation graphs

drivers_expected: Continued urban housing demand and premium housing sales support the medium-term thesis
Commercial real estate benefiting from data-centre/warehousing capex is an emerging structural driver
Festive/year-end season historically supportive of housing sales momentum

drivers: General risk-off period affected consumer discretionary/services names
Services sector saw ₹3,237 crore of outflows in H1 December 2025, among the larger sectoral outflows that fortnight
Consumer Services and Realty were among sectors that returned to inflows in Feb 2026 after heavy Jan26 selling, part of the broader rotation-driven rebound
Consumer Services recorded ₹5,338 crore of outflows in H1 April 2026, the second-largest sectoral outflow that fortnight after BFSI
Demand-compression risk from energy-price pass-through flagged as the key driver of the selling

Services (a related but distinct category) was actually one of the three sectors with net inflows in May 2026, showing a split between consumer-discretionary services (weak) and broader business/IT-adjacent services (strong)

drivers_expected: Energy-price pass-through to consumers remains the key near-term headwind if oil prices stay elevated
Festive-season discretionary spending recovery hopes into Q3/Q4
Festive and wedding-season consumption typically the strongest seasonal tailwind for consumer/discretionary services
